content:
Hello,

As suggested by someone during the plumbers conference NVMe driver in Rust presentation, is there a plan to implement the null block driver in Rust?
What's the value in a null block driver in Rust? Genuine question.
Unsure. I think the same question was raised there.

I think an NVMe driver is much higher in complexity. Writing a null driver is easier, but then if it doesn't provide any new information, it is a waste of time.

P.S. I don't have authority in any of these. All opinions are my own. :-)

I agree with what you are saying. The point that a block layer driver could be written in Rust with the same speed as C was proven with the NVMe driver, so writing null block too doesn't make sense.

I was actually looking for a project to enter the rust for Linux space, so was wondering what has been decided about the null block driver discussion.
I think when the null block driver was brought up in plumbers, it was a suggestion to use null block as the backend (instead of a real hardware), in that software difference would be more obvious

That said, investigating rewrite of any piece of the kernel might be helpful, at least would help us understand more. If you have some background in block layers and try to get to know how Rust-for-Linux works, I see no reason you cannot start with the null block driver. Although you should note that the work might not be merged eventually.

Boqun Feng said:

I think when the null block driver was brought up in plumbers, it was a suggestion to use null block as the backend (instead of a real hardware), in that software difference would be more obvious

Yes, that was the reason, to take the HW out of the loop for performance evaluations. I think it would be a nice thing to have though. Good example code, and fine for driving abstraction development.
